behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is whether the AI model correctly extracts all key terms — not a module handoff issue. LLM-DEP: A stubbed extractor always returns a fixed list, hiding the model's actual recall. The specific failure — missing an obligation phrased indirectly, or a date embedded in prose — only appears with a real model call. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior limits correctness to routing or payload format; the assertion is about completeness of term extraction, which is AI judgment. Integration or lower is disqualified because a deterministic stub replaces the model's actual output with a constant and would always pass.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real legal contract (with known parties, dates, obligations, and penalties) to the live clause extractor. Assert that every expected key term is present in the returned extraction, covering each category: parties, dates, obligations, penalties. Run with multiple contracts covering varied phrasings to probe recall.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Indirect or euphemistic' phrasing is the key qualifier. A stub always returns the same risk flags regardless of input wording; only a real model call will reveal whether the assessor can reason through softened language. MOCK-HIDE: A deterministic mock always passes because it returns the expected flags by construction, hiding model failures on subtle phrasing. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior explicitly tests model judgment quality ('even when... indirect or euphemistic'), not infrastructure. No text in the behavior limits it to routing or retry logic, so Integration is disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Present the real risk assessor with contract clauses containing indirect or euphemistic risky language (e.g., 'the party may elect not to fulfill' instead of 'the party may breach') against a fixed risk policy. Assert that each euphemistic clause is flagged as problematic. Contrast with clearly benign clauses that must not be flagged as false positives.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Whether a contract 'meets all applicable regulatory requirements' is a qualitative judgment that depends on the model's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific law and its ability to apply it to specific contract text. A stub returns a fixed pass/fail, masking any model errors. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the compliance checker always produces the expected verdict, making the test vacuous. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior is about correctness of compliance determination, not about how the checker is invoked or how responses are routed — Integration cannot surface this class of failure.
behaviors.test_description: Submit contracts to the live compliance checker with a specific jurisdiction configured. Include one compliant contract and one with a known missing requirement. Assert that the checker correctly returns compliant for the first and non-compliant (with the specific missing requirement identified) for the second. Test with at least two distinct jurisdictions.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives inside the merge function's conflict-resolution logic, not at a module boundary. Given two structured reviewer outputs containing conflicting assessments for the same clause, does the merge function preserve both with correct attribution? This is deterministic algorithmic logic. LLM-DEP: The merge step does not make AI decisions; it operates on structured data already produced by reviewers. Reviewer outputs can be constructed directly as model instances. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed. (b) Merge logic does not depend on AI output. (c) No chained agentic steps. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in this behavior requires the real reviewers to run — the failure ('both assessments not preserved') is fully reproducible by calling the merge function with hand-crafted conflicting inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two reviewer output model instances for the same clause ID — one with assessment 'standard' attributed to the extractor, one with 'high risk' attributed to the risk assessor. Call the merge function directly with these two inputs. Assert that the unified report contains both assessments, each with the correct reviewer attribution, and that neither is dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The pipeline dispatches to three independent AI reviewers in parallel (three agentic steps) and then merges. This is multiple agentic steps in sequence. LLM-DEP: 'Without losing any individual reviewer's findings' requires that all three real reviewers return findings and that the collection/merge layer preserves them. A stub for any reviewer replaces real findings with a constant, hiding pipeline bugs that only emerge when real, variably-structured model outputs flow through. REAL-INFRA: Three real model calls must complete and their results collected — real concurrency and real response payloads are exercised. REJECTION-GATE: Behavior explicitly names all three reviewers running and the merge step, establishing a multi-step agentic pipeline as the minimum scope; anything below Workflow would stub at least one agentic step and hide cross-step data-loss bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real legal contract to the full pipeline with all three live reviewers enabled. Assert that the unified report contains at least one finding from each reviewer (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker), that no reviewer's output section is empty or missing, and that the report structure is valid. Run the pipeline end-to-end without any reviewer stubbed out.
